title: Romance of the Three Kingdoms / Chapter 7 — Yuan Shao Fights Gongsun Zan at the River Pan / Sun Jian Crosses the Great River to Attack Liu Biao
text:
At the close of the last chapter,Sun Jianand his men were surrounded by the forces ofLiu Biao. It was only thanks to the selfless efforts of his three captainsCheng Pu,Huang Gai, andHan Dang, not to mention the loss of half his troops, thatSun Jianwas able to seize a way out and make it back to the Southland. From then on,Sun JianandLiu Biaowere bitter enemies.

After departingLuoyangto return east,Yuan Shaoand his troops were camped inHenei commandery.Yuan Shaolacked adequate supplies for his army, and theShepherdofJi province,Han Fu, kept sending agents with grain to sustain him.Yuan Shao’s adviser,Feng Ji, proposed, “A real man would claim the whole realm as his own; why do you choose to depend upon another for food? IfJi provinceis really so rich in wealth and goods, General, why not seize it for yourself?”

Yuan Shaotold him, “I haven’t thought of a good plan yet.”

“You could have someone secretly bring a letter toGongsun Zanordering him to advance and attackJi province, promising that you will attack from the other direction as well.”Feng Jiproposed. “That would definitely causeGongsun Zanto raise his troops, and when as gormless a man asHan Fuperceives such a threat, he will surely offer to turn control of the province over to you, General. You would be able to take the whole thing without even lifting a finger.”

Yuan Shaowas very taken with this idea, sending the letter toGongsun Zan, and whenGongsun Zangot the letter and read the offer to attackJi provincetogether and split it in half, he was so delighted that he mustered his soldiers that very same day. Little did he know thatYuan Shaohad also secretly sent an agent to informHan Fuof his attack.

Han Fuhastily summoned his advisersXun ChenandXin Pingto ask them what to do.Xun Chentold him, “Gongsun Zanwill be leading the soldiers of the Yan and Dai regions on a direct march to attack us, and we will not be able to withstand their onslaught. And withLiu Bei,Guan Yu, andZhang Feihelping him as well, he will be a difficult foe to counter. But there isYuan Benchu, whose cunning and boldness surpasses others and who has his own wide assortment of famous generals as well. General, why not ask him to manage the affairs of the province together with you? He would certainly be good to you, while you would no longer need to fearGongsun Zan.”

Han Fuwas thus just about to send one of his officials, Guan Chun, to inviteYuan Shaoto enter the province. But his Chief Clerk,Geng Wu, objected. “General,Yuan Shaois an isolated commander with an exhausted army who depends upon your sustenance like a babe cradled in its mother’s arms; as soon as the flow of milk stops, he will swiftly starve. Why would you wish to entrust the affairs of the province to him? You would only be bringing in a tiger among sheep.”

Han Fureplied, “I myself used to be an official serving the Yuan clan, and I am well aware that my talents and abilities do not measure up toBenchu. Besides, even the ancients would yield power in the presence of a sage. Why must you gentlemen be so paranoid?”

Geng Wusighed, “Ji provinceis finished!”.

More than thirty ofHan Fu’s officials outright resigned their posts and left.Geng Wuand Guan Chun chose to hide outside the provincial capital to await the arrival ofYuan Shao.

They had not long to wait, for several days laterYuan Shaoarrived with his soldiers. The pair drew their blades and sprang from their hiding places, hoping to assassinateYuan Shao. YetYuan Shao’s generalYan LiangbeheadedGeng Wuwhere he stood whileWen Chouhacked Guan Chun to death.

OnceYuan Shaoentered the city, he appointedHan Fuas General Who Rouses Might. Yet he wrested away all ofHan Fu’s actual authority by dividing the provincial roles among his own subordinatesTian Feng,Ju Shou,Xu You, andFeng Ji. Realizing that it was too late for regrets and remorse,Han Fuabandoned his family and rode away to take refuge with the Administrator of Chenliu,Zhang Miao.

Upon learning thatJi provincewas actually already inYuan Shao’s possession,Gongsun Zansent his younger brotherGongsun Yueto see him, hoping thatYuan Shaowould give him his share.Yuan ShaotoldGongsun Yue, “Have your brother come and see me himself; I have matters to discuss with him.”

Gongsun Yuethus took his leave. Yet less than fifty li from the city, a company of men rushed in from the side of the road, declaring, “We are the household generals ofAssisting ChancellorDong Zhuo!” And they shot a flurry of arrows, killingGongsun Yue. His followers fled back toGongsun Zanand informed him of his brother’s death.

Gongsun Zanraged, “Yuan Shaofooled me into raising my troops to attackHan Fujust so he could take the province for himself. Now he’s had his own agents pose asDong Zhuo’s troops to shoot my brother to death. How can I fail to avenge these injustices?”

He raised all the troops he could muster and began cutting his way towardsJi province. WhenYuan Shaolearned thatGongsun Zan’s army was approaching, he also led his army out to intercept him. The two armies met at the bridge at the Pan River, withYuan Shaoon the east bank andGongsun Zanon the west. From atop his horse on the bridge,Gongsun Zanyelled out, “You swindler, how dare you cheat me?”

Yuan Shaoalso steered his horse to his end of the bridge and, pointing atGongsun Zan, replied, “Han Fuwillingly yieldedJi provinceto me because he was unequal to the rule. What concern is it of yours?”

Gongsun Zanreplied, “I once acclaimed you as leader of our alliance, thinking you to be a loyal and righteous man. Now I see you as you really are: a lout with a wolf’s heart and a dog’s behavior. How can you even show your face before the world?”

“Who will capture him?” criedYuan Shaoin a rage.

The words were hardly out of his mouth whenWen Chougalloped out with his spear set, charging straight down the bridge.Gongsun Zanrode up from his side to take onWen Chou. But after about ten bouts, feeling that he could not keep up,Gongsun Zanbroke off and fled.Wen Choupressed his advantage to chase after him, and thoughGongsun Zantried to take shelter behind his own lines,Wen Choucame bounding after and rushed through the enemy formation, charging hither and thither. Four powerful officers serving underGongsun Zancame up to confront him, butWen Chousent one tumbling with a thrust of his spear and the other three fled. ThenWen Chouburst through the other side ofGongsun Zan’s men. Spying a mountain valley nearby,Gongsun Zanmade a break for it asWen Chouurged on his horse and bellowed, “Dismount and surrender at once!”

Gongsun Zan’s bow and quiver dropped from his shoulders, his helmet fell to the ground and his hair streamed freely behind him as he rode in and out between the sloping hills. Then his steed stumbled and he was thrown, rolling over and over to the foot of the slope.

Wen Chouswiftly poised his spear for the killing thrust. But suddenly from behind the left side of a grassy knoll came a leader of youthful mien, riding hard and holding a sturdy spear. He rode directly atWen Chou, whileGongsun Zancrawled up the slope to look on.

This young man was of middle height with bushy brows and large eyes, a broad face and a heavy jaw: a youth of commanding presence. Even after exchanging fifty or sixty heavy blows withWen Chou, the young man was still holding his own. At last a rescue force came up, andWen Chouturned and rode away. The youth did not pursue.

Gongsun Zanscrambled down the hill and asked the young fellow who he was.

The youth bowed low and replied, “I amZhao YunstyledZilong, fromZhendinginChangshan.

“I was actually a subordinate ofYuan Shao, but when I saw how he had no intention to be loyal to his sovereign or to rescue the people, I abandoned him and, intending to offer my services to you, came upon you here.”

Overjoyed,Gongsun ZantookZhao Yunback to his camp with him, where they at once busied themselves with preparations for a new battle.

The next day,Gongsun Zanprepared to fight by dividing his army into two groups, like the wings of a bird. He had some five thousand cavalry, most of them mounted on white horses as was his custom, for in his earlier days during campaigns against the Qiang people he had become known as the “White Horse General” for always placing white horses in his vanguard; in fact, the Qiang would tend to flee at the very sight of them, inspiring him to always keep a great number on hand.

Yuan ShaoorderedYan LiangandWen Chouto lead his own vanguard: each had a thousand archers and crossbowmen, and they too split into two groups on the left and right with orders to shoot at their counterparts inGongsun Zan’s army. He also orderedQu Yito lead the center with a formation of eight hundred archers and fifteen thousand infantry.Yuan Shaohimself led tens of thousands of horse and foot in the rear to act as reserve.

SinceZhao Yunhad only just entered his service andGongsun Zanwas not yet certain of his intentions, he orderedZhao Yunto command a division in the rear while assigning his leading generalYan Gangto lead the vanguard.Gongsun Zanhimself led the rest of his troops in the center. He took his place on horseback on the bridge beside an enormous red standard, on which was displayed the words “Commanding General” in gold embroidery.

All through the morning, the drums rolled as though announcing the attack, butYuan Shao’s army made no move;Qu Yihad even ordered his archers to remain concealed behind their shields with their bows loose, not shooting until they heard the burst of the signal bombs. At lengthYan Gangbegan beating his own drums and whipping up a frenzy, marching straight towardsQu Yi, yet even asYan Gang’s troops drew closer and closerQu Yi’s soldiers stayed low without stirring. Then just asYan Gang’s soldiers were about to reach them, the signal bombs went off. At once the eight hundred archers began launching volleys of arrows.Yan Gangquickly ordered a withdrawal, butQu Yislapped his horse and twirled his blade and in an instant he had hackedYan Gangdown from his horse.Gongsun Zan’s army suffered heavy casualties; though the two wings sought to save the center, they were held in place by arrows shot byYan Liang’s andWen Chou’s archers across from them.Yuan Shao’s soldiers then began to advance, cutting their way across the bridge, andQu Yirode forward and even took the head ofGongsun Zan’s standard bearer and cut down his embroidered banner. Seeing the fall of his banner,Gongsun Zanturned his steed and galloped away off the bridge.

Qu Yinow led the troops to charge towardsGongsun Zan’s rear division. But out rodeZhao Yun, prancing forth with spear ready to strike; he galloped straight forQu Yi, and after a handful of boutsZhao Yunthrust his spear throughQu Yiand sent him to the ground. ThenZhao Yunon his own plunged straight into the Yuan soldiers, charging this way and that as though he had the field to himself. Heartened by these displays of prowess,Gongsun Zanrallied his soldiers and turned them back to the attack, and now it wasYuan Shao’s troops who began taking heavy losses.

From his position at the rear of his own army,Yuan Shaohad only just heard the report thatQu Yihad taken the head ofGongsun Zan’s standard bearer and was in hot pursuit of his beaten troops. Assuming that the battle had already been won,Yuan ShaobroughtTian Fengto ride out with just a few hundred halberdiers and a few dozen mounted archers to observe the end of the fighting.

“Ah,Gongsun Zan,”Yuan Shaosaid as he laughed, “how worthless you really are!”

Yet just as he was making this remark,Zhao Yunsuddenly came bounding into view.Yuan Shao’s archers quickly tried to form up to shoot him, butZhao Yunstabbed several of them with his spear and the rest all fled. And hot on his heels wereGongsun Zan’s rallied soldiers, who spread out to surroundYuan Shaoand his party.

In a frightTian Fengexclaimed, “My lord, protect yourself within this wall!”

ButYuan Shaoonly threw his helmet to the ground and announced, “A real man will hold the line even in the face of death! Who’d be so craven as to hide in a wall?”

Inspired by this bold speech,Yuan Shao’s soldiers resolved to fight even at the cost of their own lives, andZhao Yuncould not break through them. Soon the rest ofYuan Shao’s main body came up whileYan Liangdiverted his wing to reinforce his lord as well, and another bloody melee ensued; it was allZhao Yuncould do to protectGongsun Zanlong enough to slip through the encirclement and return to the bridge.Yuan Shao’s army squeezedGongsun Zan’s soldiers back onto the bridge, and as they once again forced a crossing, countless numbers ofGongsun Zan’s soldiers fell into the river and drowned.

Yuan Shaowas now leading the pursuit at the head of his army. Yet before they had traveled five li, a great deal of shouting rang out from behind a hill and a new body of soldiers marched onto the field, led by three commanders: it wasXuande,Guan Yunchang, andZhang Yide.

AtPingyuanthey had learned of the struggle betweenGongsun ZanandYuan Shaoand had at once set out to help. Confronted by these three brothers, each with his own powerful weapon and all charging hard and coming straight for him,Yuan Shaowas so caught off guard that his very soul seemed to ascend to the heavens; he dropped his precious sword to the ground and hastily turned his horse to flee, while his soldiers all fought for dear life to get back across the bridge again.Gongsun Zanchose to pull back his own soldiers as well, and they withdrew to their camp.

AfterXuande,Guan Yu, andZhang Feihad finished explaining themselves,Gongsun Zantold them, “If you had not come all this way to save us,Xuande, we would have been sorely pressed.”

Gongsun Zanalso took the opportunity to introduce the brothers toZhao Yun.Xuandeat once became so smitten with the man that he resolved never to be apart from him.

Having shifted his own camp,Yuan Shaoremained on the defensive and no longer offered battle. The two sides thus remained in stalemate for more than a month. Someone brought news of the standoff between them to Chang’an to informDong Zhuoof the situation.

Li Ruadvised him, “Yuan ShaoandGongsun Zanare both still powerful leaders of our time. Since they are locked in bloody conflict with one another at the Pan River, you should issue an edict in the name of theSon of Heavenand send an envoy to have them make peace with one another. Both of them will appreciate the court’s intervention and will be sure to obey you.”

Dong Zhuowas very pleased by this idea, and the next day he sent offGrand Tutor,Ma Midi, andMinister Chamberlain,Zhao Qi, bearing such an edict. As these two ministers approached the scene of the fighting,Yuan Shaotraveled out a hundred li to welcome their arrival, bowing twice in reverence as he accepted the imperial edict. The following day, the envoys went toGongsun Zan’s camp to convey their orders to him as well, andGongsun Zansent his own envoy toYuan Shaowith a letter agreeing to peace between them. The two ministers thus returned to the capital to report the success of their mission, while that same dayGongsun Zanled his army back to his own domain, having taken the opportunity to submit a petition asking thatLiu Xuandebe confirmed in his position as Chancellor of Pingyuan.

AsXuandewas bidding farewell toZhao Yun, he held the man’s hand with tears in his eyes, not willing to see him go.

Zhao Yunsighed, “I was misled back then into thinking thatGongsun Zanwas a noble hero, yet from what I’ve seen of him since, he’s no better thanYuan Shao!”

“You must submit to serving him for now,”Xuandetold him. “There will come a day when we meet again.”

Both men wept freely as they separated.

At Nanyang, when he had heard thatYuan Shaohad just acquiredJi province, his brotherYuan Shuhad sent agents to ask him for a thousand horses. YetYuan Shaohad denied him, enragingYuan Shu, and from then on there was an enmity between them. At the same time,Yuan Shuhad also sent agents toJing provinceto askLiu Biaoto loan him two hundred measures of grain, yetLiu Biaohad denied him as well, thus earningYuan Shu’s ire. Thus at this time,Yuan Shusecretly sent a letter toSun Jianto try to get him to campaign againstLiu Biao. The letter ran like this:

“WhenLiu Biaoblocked your way home before, it was part of my elder brotherBenchu’s scheme. And by now,Benchuis even in secret talks withLiu Biaoto launch a surprise attack against the Southland itself. You should strike the first blow by swiftly mustering your troops to campaign againstLiu Biao; at the same time I shall captureBenchufor you, so that you will have revenge against both your enemies. And you shall takeJing province, while I getJi province. Make no mistake!”

“Liu Biaoit is then!” saidSun Jianas he finished reading this letter. “He certainly did bar my way home back then; if I don’t seize this chance to avenge myself now, who knows how long I’ll have to wait?”

Sun Jiansummoned his captains, includingCheng Pu,Huang Gai, andHan Dang, to further discuss the idea.

Cheng Puwarned him, “Yuan Shuis full of guile; don’t be so quick to believe him.”

“I’m the one who wants to get revenge,” saidSun Jian. “You think I neededYuan Shuto help convince me of that?”

And he swiftly dispatchedHuang Gaito go on ahead to prepare a fleet of warships to sail up the Yangzi, with most equipped to carry army gear and provisions while the largest were equipped to carry warhorses.Sun Jianset a date for when his army would depart for the campaign.

As the earliest details ofSun Jian’s preparations began to travel up the Yangzi, they were reported toLiu Biao. Astonished,Liu Biaoquickly summoned his civil and military officials to discuss the matter. ButKuai Liangadvised him, “There is no need to worry so much. Just haveHuang Zulead the garrison at Jiangxia ahead as a vanguard, my lord, while you lead the bulk of this region’s troops as his reserve.Sun Jianwill be so obliged to cross rivers and wade through lakes to get here that what damage will he be able to do?”

Liu Biaoagreed, and he orderedHuang Zuto make his own preparations whileLiu Biaoarranged to bring up the main army behind him.

By this time,Sun Jianhad six sons and a daughter. His first wifeLady Wuhad given birth to his first four sons:

Sun Ce, styledBofu

Sun Quan, styledZhongmou

Sun Yi, styledShubi

Sun Kuang, styledJizuo

And his second wifeLady Wu’s younger sister had given birth to the fifth son and the daughter:

Sun Lang, styledZao’an

Sun Ren

The sixth son,Sun ShaostyledGongli, had been adopted from the Yu family.Sun Jianalso had a younger brother,Sun JingstyledYoutai.

AsSun Jianwas about to leave on this expedition,Sun Jingbrought along all ofSun Jian’s sons to stand in a line before his steed and bow down while he tried to talk him out of going: “Dong Zhuois monopolizing power over the court, the Son of Heaven is a powerless youth, and practically everywhere within the Four Seas is in total chaos, with warlords everywhere trying to seize power. We have only just brought some measure of stability to the Southland as it is. Raising so vast an army just to avenge a petty personal slight is hardly the right thing to do. Elder Brother, think this through.”

ButSun Jianonly replied, “Little Brother, say no more. If I intend to flaunt my will across the whole breadth of the realm someday, how can I stand to let any such insult slide?”

“If you insist on going, Father,” said the eldest sonSun Ce, “I want to go with you.”

Sun Jiangave his consent, and father and son got onto their boat together. Then the fleet was away, pushing on forFancheng.

Huang Zuhad placed archers and crossbowmen along the river bank, and as soon as they sawSun Jian’s ships pulling up to the shore they began shooting wild flurries of arrows at them. Ordering his forces not to make any rash movements,Sun Jianhad his soldiers remain under cover inside the ships while simply having ships approach the shore to attract the attention of the archers before backing off again; they kept this strategy up for three days, making dozens of false landings along the bank.Huang Zu’s soldiers, thinking of nothing more than shooting their arrows whenever a ship approached, eventually exhausted all their arrows, andSun Jianhelped himself to the arrows which had been shot into his ships, thereby gaining more than a hundred thousand of them. Then one day when a fair wind arrived,Sun Jiancommanded his men to shoot coordinated volleys at the shore, and unable to hold their ground,Huang Zu’s archers could only flee.

At lastSun Jianhad his troops scale the bank, andCheng PuandHuang Gaisplit off two divisions to make straight forHuang Zu’s camp whileHan Dangfollowed behind with a general advance by the rest of the army. They attacked from three directions, greatly defeatingHuang Zu, who abandoned Fancheng and retreated into Dengcheng.

OrderingHuang Gaito remain behind to guard the fleet,Sun Jianled the army to pursue the enemy.Huang Zucame out of his city and drew up for battle in the field. OnceSun Jianhad arranged his own army’s formation, he rode out to wait beneath his battle standard at the gate of the camp.Sun Cestood astride his own horse beside his father, clad in a full suit of armor and holding his spear at the ready.

Huang Zurode out with two captains, Zhang Hu of Jiangxia andChen Shengof Xiangyang. Flourishing his whip,Huang Zucalled out, “You verminous rebels from the Southland, how dare you invade the domain of a relative of the ruling house?”

And he promptly ordered Zhang Hu to ride out to demand a duel.Han Dangemerged fromSun Jian’s formation to meet the challenge. The two riders clashed for more than thirty bouts beforeChen Sheng, observing that Zhang Hu’s strength was fading, flew out on his own horse to aid him. Yet whenSun Cesaw whatChen Shengwas up to, he laid aside his spear, reached for his bow, and shot an arrow; no sooner had the bowstring twanged thenChen Shengfell from his horse,Sun Cehaving shot him square in the face. Zhang Hu choked back a cry of panic as he watched his companion fall to the ground; he failed to react to the next blow in time, and a slash ofHan Dang’s sword took off half his head.

Cheng Puthen galloped straight ahead, intending to grabHuang Zuwhile he was still exposed, butHuang Zuthrew off his helmet, slipped from his warhorse, and ran for his life by blending in among his infantry.Sun Jianled on the attack and dealt the enemy a smart defeat. He advanced up to the line of the Han River near Xiangyang, while orderingHuang Gaito bring the fleet further up the Yangzi to berth at the mouth of the Han River.

Huang Zugathered up his defeated men and went to meet withLiu Biaoin Xiangyang, arguing that they could not faceSun Jian. Unnerved by this opinion,Liu Biaocalled a council and askedKuai Liangfor his advice.Kuai Liangsaid, “Our newly defeated men have no heart for fighting now; the only option is to fortify our defenses and avoid battle. But so long as we secretly send someone to request help fromYuan Shao, this siege will break up on its own.”

ButCai Maoexclaimed, “Zirou’s advice is just plain bad! The enemy soldiers are beneath our walls and their commander is at the Han River; how can we just fold our hands and wait for death? I’m hardly talented, but just let me take an army out of the city and I’ll finish this in one battle.”

Liu Biaogranted his permission, andCai Maoled more than ten thousand soldiers to march out of Xiangyang and form up at the Xian Hills.Sun Jianled the invaders, now flush with success, to march right up to confront them. WhenCai Maorode out from his formation,Sun Jianasked his generals, “That man is the brother ofLiu Biao’s new wife; who will join me in capturing him?”

Cheng Puset his iron-spined spear and rode out, clashing withCai Mao. Yet within just a few bouts,Cai Maoran away in defeat.Sun Jianthen led his army to advance, killing so many foes that their corpses littered the field.Cai Maofled back into Xiangyang.

Kuai Liangproposed that asCai Maohad ignored his proper strategy and had caused a great defeat, according to military law he ought to be beheaded. ButLiu Biaowas unwilling to punish the brother of his newly-wedded wife.

Sun Jiansplit his army into four groups and laid siege to Xiangyang, launching repeated assaults. One day, a fierce gale sprang up and snapped in half the pole in the center ofSun Jian’s camp bearing his standard marked “Commanding General”.

Han Dangremarked, “This is no good omen. Perhaps we should conclude the campaign for now.”

ButSun Jianreplied, “I have won every battle and Xiangyang will be mine any day now. Why should I disband my soldiers just because the wind broke a flagstaff?”

And rather than heedHan Dang’s advice, he only assaulted Xiangyang with even greater ferocity.

Kuai LiangtoldLiu Biao, “When I was watching the night sky for any changes in the heavens, I noticed that a general’s star was about to fall, and judging by the star’s position in the sky, it is surely meant to correspond toSun Jian. My lord, you should send a letter toYuan Shaoat once to ask him to assist us.”

Liu Biaowrote out the letter, then asked who was daring enough to break through the siege to deliver it. At once someone offered to go, a mighty general namedLü Gong.Kuai Liangtold him, “Since you are brave enough to go, listen to my advice: take five hundred soldiers with you, as many good archers as you can find, and charge out through the enemy’s siege lines to make for the Xian Hills. The enemy will undoubtedly pursue you, so send off a hundred of your men to go up the hills and look for rocks to use while sending a hundred more with bows and crossbows to hide in the woods. Once the enemy’s pursuit comes near, do not take the swiftest path to get away but follow a winding and circuitous route until you’ve led them to where your ambush troops are, then let fly all the stones and arrows together. If you fight and win there, then send up some crimson column flares and the rest of our soldiers will immediately march out of the city to follow up the victory. And if no one pursues you after all, don’t light the flares but just get going. The moon won’t be very clear tonight, so leave the city at dusk.”

Lü Gongspent the rest of the day carrying out his preparations and choosing whom he would bring with him. Once dusk arrived, he secretly opened the east gate of the city and led his force out.Sun Jianhad been in his tent, but as soon as he heard the commotion he mounted his horse and rode out of his camp with barely thirty riders to see what was happening. An officer told him, “A body of soldiers fought their way out and went off towards the Xian Hills.” Without even waiting to assemble his generals,Sun Jianrode off in hot pursuit with just the thirty riders.

By the time thatSun Jian’s band had the enemy in sight,Lü Gonghad already sent away the two groups to the hills and the woods to prepare their ambushes.Sun Jian’s horse was especially swift, and he rode on ahead of his escorts to approach the enemy, shouting, “Stop right there!”Lü Gongturned his horse around and rode atSun Jianas if to duel him, but after just a single bout he swerved about again and rode away, taking a road down among the hills.Sun Jianchased after him, but eventually lost sight of his foe.

Just asSun Jianturned to come back out of the hills, the sound of gongs suddenly rang out, and a mess of stones tumbled down from atop the hills while flurries of arrows shot out of the woods. Caught between this crossfire,Sun Jianwas struck by so many stones and arrows that he was left a mess of blood and guts, and both he and his horse perished among the Xian Hills. He had been only thirty-six years old.

Lü Gongpicked off and killed all thirty ofSun Jian’s escort riders as well, then sent up the crimson column flares. At this signal,Huang Zu,Kuai Yue, andCai Maoeach led a division out of the city to charge the enemy lines, and the army of the Southland was thrown into general confusion.

WhenHuang Gaiheard such commotion from the direction of Xiangyang that it seemed to shake the very heavens, he led the soldiers who had stayed with the fleet to march towards the sound of battle. He happened to run straight intoHuang Zu’s force, and within two bouts he tookHuang Zucaptive.

Cheng Puwas protectingSun Ceand desperately searching for a way out when he happened uponLü Gong. He rushed his steed forward and within just a few bouts had piercedLü Gongwith his spear and sent him tumbling. The slaughter between the two sides lasted all night, until morning came and they each broke off to reorganize andLiu Biaoordered his army back into the city.

Only onceSun Cereached the Han River again did he learn that his father had been shot to death by a flurry of arrows and that his body had been taken byLiu Biao’s soldiers back into Xiangyang. A great wail burst out of him, and the army was filled with weeping and tears.

“How can I return home leaving my father’s corpse with them?” criedSun Ce.

Huang Gaisaid, “We haveHuang Zuhere as our prisoner. Let’s have someone enter the city and discuss peace, giving up our prisoner for our lord’s body.”

He had barely finished speaking when one of the army clerks,Huan Jie, came forward to say, “I have a history withLiu Biao. I’m willing to enter the city as the envoy.”Sun Ceagreed.

Huan Jiethen entered Xiangyang and met withLiu Biao, explaining his mission.Liu Biaotold him, “I have already prepared a coffin here forWentai’s body and head. If you will release and send backHuang Zuat once, then each of us can disband our armies and refrain from violating the other’s territory any further.”

Huan Jieperformed obeisance to thank him, and planned to take his leave. But from the foot of the stairsKuai Liangcame in to say, “Don’t, don’t! Let me speak and I will see to it that not even a single breastplate of the Southland army will return. First beheadHuan Jie, then employ my means.”

And so it was:

In pursuing an enemy,Sun Jianlaid down his life;

In seeking peace,Huan Jietoo courts danger.

What wasHuan Jie’s fate? The next chapter will reveal.
